title: Indicators of Capacity and Inflation Pressures for Canada
summary: The Bank of Canada publishes a comprehensive dashboard of capacity and inflation pressure indicators, updated one day after each rate announcement. Latest data shows Canadian CPI inflation at 1.8% year-over-year in February 2026, below the 2% target, with multiple measures of core inflation (CPI-trim 2.3%, CPI-median 2.3%, CPI-common 2.4%) also running near or slightly above target. The output gap is estimated between -0.8% and -1.6% depending on methodology, indicating below-potential economic activity. Labour market slack is evident with unemployment at 6.7% and wage growth decelerating across most measures to 2.6-3.9% year-over-year.
key_points: CPI inflation printed at 1.8% year-over-year in February 2026, down from 2.2% in Q4 2025 and below the 2% target
Output gap estimates range from -0.8% (MPR integrated framework) to -1.6% (extended multivariate filter), indicating persistent economic slack
Unemployment rate held at 6.7% in February 2026 with labour market indicator also at 6.7, above the pre-pandemic range
Wage growth decelerated to 2.6% year-over-year in February 2026 (LFS-Micro measure), down from 3.9% in Q2 2024
Unit labour costs increased 2.1% year-over-year in Q4 2025, below the 3.8% peak in Q2 2024
24% of firms expect price increases exceeding 3% over the next six months (Q4 data), down from a peak of 45% in Q1 2025
why_it_matters: This dashboard is a key input for Bank of Canada monetary policy decisions. The data suggests inflation has moderated to near-target levels while the economy operates with slack (negative output gap, above-normal unemployment, decelerating wages). This combination supports the case for continued accommodative monetary policy. Fixed announcement dates for rate decisions follow one day after these tables are updated, making this a reference document for policy anticipation.
macro_context: Canadian inflation has returned to near the 2% target after being elevated in 2022-2023. Multiple measures of core inflation (CPI-trim, CPI-median, CPI-common) cluster around 2.3-2.4%, suggesting the disinflationary trend is broad-based. The economy shows persistent slack with output gap estimates indicating below-potential GDP. Labour market conditions have loosened, with unemployment at 6.7% and participation rate declining to 64.9%. Wage growth has decelerated meaningfully from 5%+ peaks to 2.6-3.9% depending on measure, reducing inflationary pressure from labour costs.

human_review.notes: This is a data reference dashboard rather than a standalone analysis. The source provides underlying indicator data that feeds into monetary policy decisions. Published date of March 19, 2026 is recent (within 2 weeks of TODAY), making it highly relevant. The data supports a narrative of moderating inflation and economic slack consistent with Bank of Canada's mandate.

notes: This source is a data dashboard rather than a policy statement. It aggregates multiple economic indicators used by the Bank of Canada to assess capacity pressures and inflation trajectory. The most recent data point is February 2026 for monthly indicators and Q1 2026 for quarterly data. This is a supporting reference document rather than a standalone macro catalyst, but contains highly relevant data for understanding the Canadian economic outlook and potential monetary policy direction.
